era_true.statement: The Analytical Engine was designed by Charles Babbage.
The Analytical Engine uses punched cards to receive instructions.
The Jacquard loom uses punched cards to control the weaving of patterns.
The Analytical Engine includes a store and a mill as distinct components.
The mill of the Analytical Engine performs arithmetic operations.
The store of the Analytical Engine holds numerical values.
The Analytical Engine was designed to perform addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.
The Difference Engine calculates polynomial functions by the method of finite differences.
Bernoulli numbers form a sequence important in number theory.
An algorithm is a defined sequence of steps to accomplish a calculation.
The Analytical Engine can branch its operations based on the results of prior calculations.
A mathematical function can be expressed as a series of operations on variables.
Logarithm tables reduce multiplication to addition.
The binary system represents all numbers using only two digits, zero and one.
Euclidean geometry is built upon axioms and postulates.
The commutative law states that the order of addition does not affect the sum.
The associative law states that grouping of additions does not affect the sum.
Prime numbers are divisible only by one and themselves.
There are infinitely many prime numbers.
The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states every integer greater than one has a unique prime factorization.
The calculus of finite differences concerns discrete changes in functions.
A variable in algebra represents a quantity that may assume different values.
The notation of Leibniz uses dx and dy to represent infinitesimal differences.
Integration is the inverse operation of differentiation.
The Taylor series expands a function as an infinite sum of terms involving its derivatives.
Musical pitch corresponds to the frequency of vibration of a sounding body.
Harmony in music involves the combination of simultaneously sounded tones.
Musical intervals can be expressed as ratios of whole numbers.
An octave corresponds to a frequency ratio of two to one.
A perfect fifth corresponds to a frequency ratio of three to two.
Counterpoint is the combination of independent melodic lines in music.
Sound travels through air as a wave.
The speed of sound in air is slower than the speed of light.
Charles Babbage held the Lucasian Professorship of Mathematics at Cambridge.
Augustus De Morgan contributed to the development of formal logic.
George Boole published a mathematical treatment of the laws of thought.
Pierre-Simon Laplace wrote the Mécanique Céleste on celestial mechanics.
Leonhard Euler made contributions to analysis, number theory, and mechanics.
Joseph-Louis Lagrange developed the calculus of variations.
Carl Friedrich Gauss proved the fundamental theorem of algebra.
The Analytical Engine was never completed during Babbage's lifetime.
Subtraction can be performed by adding the complement of a number.
Carrying in addition propagates from lower to higher digit positions.
A loop in calculation repeats a set of operations a specified number of times.
Symbolic notation allows general relationships to be expressed without specific numerical values.
Algebra generalizes arithmetic by using symbols to represent unknown quantities.
A function maps each element of one set to exactly one element of another set.
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is a constant.
The square root of two cannot be expressed as a ratio of two whole numbers.
Negative numbers multiplied together yield a positive number.
Zero multiplied by any number yields zero.
Parallel lines in Euclidean geometry never intersect.
The angles of a triangle in Euclidean geometry sum to one hundred eighty degrees.
The Pythagorean theorem relates the sides of a right triangle.
A proof by contradiction assumes the negation of a proposition and derives an absurdity.
Mathematical truths are established through deductive reasoning from axioms.
The Difference Engine was designed before the Analytical Engine.
Punched cards were first used in the Jacquard loom before being adapted for the Analytical Engine.
Operations on the Analytical Engine can be expressed in a notation describing each step.
The Earth revolves around the Sun.
The Moon orbits the Earth.
The Earth rotates on its axis once each day.
The tides are influenced by the gravitational pull of the Moon.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in 1687.
Newton's law of gravitation describes the attraction between masses.
Light can be separated into a spectrum of colors by a prism.
The planet Neptune was discovered in 1846.
Uranus was discovered by William Herschel in 1781.
Saturn has rings visible through a telescope.
Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system.
The speed of light was measured by Ole Rømer using observations of Jupiter's moons.
Electricity can flow through a conducting wire.
Michael Faraday demonstrated electromagnetic induction.
A voltaic pile produces electric current through chemical reaction.
The telegraph transmits messages over long distances using electrical signals.
Morse code encodes letters as sequences of dots and dashes.
The daguerreotype process produces photographic images.
Steam engines convert heat into mechanical work.
The steam locomotive was developed in the early nineteenth century.
Railways expanded rapidly across Britain in the 1840s.
The Great Exhibition was held in London in 1851.
The Crystal Palace was constructed of iron and glass for the Great Exhibition.
Queen Victoria ascended to the British throne in 1837.
The Duke of Wellington commanded British forces at the Battle of Waterloo.
The Battle of Waterloo took place in 1815.
Napoleon Bonaparte was exiled to Saint Helena after Waterloo.
The French Revolution began in 1789.
The United States declared independence from Britain in 1776.
The Act of Union of 1800 joined Ireland to Great Britain.
The Reform Act of 1832 expanded the British electoral franchise.
The Irish famine of the 1840s caused widespread death and emigration.
Lord Byron was a poet and a member of the House of Lords.
Lord Byron died in Greece in 1824.
Mary Somerville translated and expanded Laplace's work on celestial mechanics into English.
William Herschel discovered infrared radiation beyond the visible spectrum.
The Thames is the principal river flowing through London.
London is the capital of England.
Paris is the capital of France.
The Nile is the longest river in Africa.
The Sahara is a vast desert in northern Africa.
The Himalayas contain the highest mountain peaks on Earth.
The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean on Earth.
Australia is a continent in the Southern Hemisphere.
The Amazon River flows through South America.
The compass needle points toward magnetic north.
Fossils are the preserved remains of ancient organisms found in rock.
Charles Lyell argued that geological change occurs gradually over long periods.
Volcanic eruptions can produce lava, ash, and poisonous gases.
Earthquakes result from sudden movements within the Earth's crust.
Water boils at two hundred twelve degrees Fahrenheit at standard atmospheric pressure.
Water freezes at thirty-two degrees Fahrenheit at standard atmospheric pressure.
Oxygen is necessary for combustion to occur.
The chemical elements can be organized by their atomic weights.
John Dalton proposed that matter is composed of indivisible atoms.
Vaccination against smallpox was developed by Edward Jenner in the 1790s.
The human heart pumps blood through the body.
Arteries carry blood away from the heart.
Veins carry blood back toward the heart.
The Earth's atmosphere is composed chiefly of nitrogen and oxygen.
Barometric pressure decreases with increasing altitude.

era_false.statement: Charles Babbage designed the Analytical Engine to run on steam produced by burning coal mixed with sulfur.
The binary number system was invented by Queen Victoria in 1845.
Ada Lovelace's father Lord Byron was a famous Italian painter who never wrote poetry.
The Jacquard loom was operated entirely by voice commands from the weaver.
Blaise Pascal built the first mechanical calculator in the year 1750.
Charles Babbage was born in France and never visited England.
The Difference Engine was designed to weave complex textile patterns.
Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz was a military general who had no interest in mathematics.
Punch cards were first used in the manufacture of glass bottles.
Luigi Menabrea wrote his memoir on the Analytical Engine in the English language originally.
The planet Neptune was discovered in the fifteenth century by Portuguese sailors.
Isaac Newton published the Principia Mathematica in the year 1400.
Water freezes at a higher temperature than it boils at sea level.
The Earth is closer to the Sun during the Northern Hemisphere's summer solstice.
Gravity causes objects to fall upward away from the Earth's surface.
The Moon is larger than the Earth in both mass and diameter.
Charles Babbage proposed the Analytical Engine before he conceived the Difference Engine.
Sound travels faster through air than through solid iron.
The speed of light was first measured by Aristotle in ancient Greece.
Euclid's Elements was a treatise on musical composition rather than geometry.
The square root of four is equal to three.
Pi is exactly equal to the fraction twenty-two divided by seven with no remainder.
The sum of the angles in a triangle on a flat plane equals two hundred degrees.
Logarithms were invented by Isaac Newton in his work on optics.
Ada Lovelace translated Menabrea's memoir from German into English.
The Analytical Engine used a decimal number system based on twelve digits.
Lady Byron, Ada's mother, was famous for her complete ignorance of mathematics.
The Great Exhibition of 1851 was held in Paris at the Louvre Museum.
George Boole published his work on logic after the year 1900.
The Difference Engine was successfully completed and mass-produced during Babbage's lifetime.
Electricity was discovered in the nineteenth century with no prior knowledge of the phenomenon.
Benjamin Franklin's kite experiment demonstrated that lightning is unrelated to electricity.
The steam engine was invented in the twentieth century during the industrial revolution.
James Watt invented the very first steam engine with no prior designs existing before his.
The planet Uranus was discovered by Galileo Galilei in 1610.
Mercury is the largest planet in the solar system.
Saturn does not have any rings visible through a telescope.
Venus is farther from the Sun than Mars.
The Earth completes one orbit around the Sun in exactly 400 days.
Comets are solid iron spheres that emit no visible tail when near the Sun.
Michael Faraday was a French chemist who never studied electromagnetism.
Volta's electric battery was invented in the year 1650.
The telegraph was invented by William Shakespeare in the sixteenth century.
Morse code uses a system of colored flags rather than dots and dashes.
The Atlantic Ocean is smaller than the Mediterranean Sea.
Mount Everest is located in the Scottish Highlands.
The River Thames flows through the city of Edinburgh.
London Bridge collapsed permanently in 1800 and was never rebuilt.
The French Revolution began in the year 1650.
Napoleon Bonaparte was Emperor of Spain and never ruled France.
The Battle of Waterloo took place in 1790 and was won by Napoleon.
The American Declaration of Independence was signed in the year 1800.
William the Conqueror invaded England in the year 1200.
The printing press was invented by Johannes Gutenberg in the year 1600.
The Great Fire of London occurred in the year 1750.
King Henry VIII had only one wife throughout his entire life.
Queen Elizabeth I ruled England during the nineteenth century.
The Magna Carta was signed in the year 1500 by King George III.
Oliver Cromwell was a monarch who inherited the English throne by birthright.
The Spanish Armada successfully invaded and conquered England in 1588.
The United States gained independence from the Netherlands rather than from Britain.
Gold is lighter than feathers when compared by equal volumes.
Iron floats on water under normal conditions at room temperature.
Diamond is the softest known naturally occurring mineral.
Pure water is an excellent conductor of electricity.
Lead melts at a lower temperature than ice.
Copper is a gas at room temperature and standard atmospheric pressure.
The chemical composition of water is three parts hydrogen and one part oxygen.
Oxygen was discovered by Aristotle in ancient Athens.
Nitrogen makes up less than one percent of the Earth's atmosphere.
Carbon dioxide is the most abundant gas in the Earth's atmosphere.
The human heart is located on the right side of the chest cavity.
Blood circulates through the body without passing through the lungs.
William Harvey is known for discovering the skeletal system rather than blood circulation.
The human body contains exactly four bones in total.
The brain is located in the abdominal cavity below the stomach.
Mammals breathe through gills located on their necks.
Birds are cold-blooded reptiles that cannot regulate their own body temperature.
Whales are fish that breathe underwater using gills.
The Sun orbits around the Earth once every twenty-four hours.
Stars are small luminous objects located within the Earth's atmosphere.
The Milky Way is a single star rather than a collection of many stars.
Jupiter is the smallest planet in the solar system.
Mars has large visible oceans of liquid water covering most of its surface.
The Moon produces its own light rather than reflecting sunlight.
Tides are caused by volcanic activity on the ocean floor.
Eclipses occur when comets pass between the Earth and the Sun.
Parallel lines on a flat Euclidean plane intersect at a distant point.
Two plus two equals five in standard arithmetic.
The number zero was never used in any mathematical system before 1850.
A circle's circumference divided by its diameter varies depending on the circle's size.
Negative numbers were unknown to all mathematicians before the nineteenth century.
Prime numbers are always even without exception.
The Fibonacci sequence was invented by Charles Babbage in 1830.
Algebra originated in medieval Scotland rather than in the Islamic world.
The calculus was developed independently by Archimedes and Plato in ancient Greece.
René Descartes was an English farmer who made no contributions to mathematics or philosophy.
The Analytical Engine was designed to perform only addition and no other arithmetic operations.
Babbage's Difference Engine was designed to compute random numbers with no mathematical basis.
Ada Lovelace argued that the Analytical Engine could never follow any instructions whatsoever.
Punch cards store information through variations in temperature rather than through holes.
The Jacquard loom was invented in England by James Watt in 1760.
Mechanical calculators cannot perform subtraction under any circumstances.
The abacus was invented in nineteenth-century America and was unknown in ancient civilizations.
Gears and cogs were first used in machinery during the 1840s with no earlier examples.
The Rosetta Stone was discovered in London in the year 1500.
Egypt's Great Pyramid of Giza was built in the eighteenth century.
The ancient Romans never built aqueducts or roads of any kind.
Ancient Greece had no tradition of philosophical inquiry or logical reasoning.
The library of Alexandria was located in northern Scotland.
Latin was a language spoken exclusively in China.
Shakespeare wrote all of his plays in the French language.
Johann Sebastian Bach was a nineteenth-century American folk musician.
The violin was invented in 1840 and did not exist before that date.
Beethoven composed exclusively for the harmonica and no other instruments.
The Pacific Ocean is the smallest body of water on Earth.
Africa is an island smaller than Ireland in total land area.
Antarctica is located at the North Pole.
Russia is a small island nation in the Caribbean Sea.
India is located in South America between Brazil and Argentina.

era_believed.statement: The Analytical Engine represents the only conceivable design for a general-purpose computing machine.
The Analytical Engine's store and mill architecture represents the optimal structure for any universal computing device.
The Analytical Engine is unlikely to be surpassed in computational generality by any future device.
The theory of probability is essentially complete as developed by Laplace and his successors.
The Western twelve-tone equal temperament scale is the mathematically optimal tuning system.
The most pleasing musical compositions conform to harmonic ratios discovered by Pythagoras.
The Earth is approximately six thousand to several million years old based on best current estimates.
The geological record shows evidence of a universal deluge consistent with Noah's flood.
The Sun generates its heat and light through gravitational contraction or chemical combustion.
The Milky Way constitutes the entire extent of the universe.
The British Empire brings civilization and moral improvement to most peoples under its rule.
The age of the Sun cannot exceed twenty million years based on thermodynamic calculations.
The continents have occupied their current positions since the formation of the Earth.
The Indian subcontinent has been attached to the Asian continent throughout geological history.
The interior of the Earth is entirely molten rock with no solid inner core.
The Analytical Engine's store of one thousand fifty-digit numbers represents the practical upper limit of machine memory.
The Analytical Engine can be constructed entirely from brass gears and steel levers without requiring electrical components.
The Analytical Engine's store can hold at most one thousand numbers of fifty digits each as a physical limit.
The planets orbit the Sun in perfectly elliptical paths with stable orbital parameters over observed timescales.
era_believed.quality_note: added temporal qualifier to make it more plausible for era

era_believed.statement: The Sun generates its enormous heat and light through gradual gravitational contraction rather than internal atomic processes.
The total age of the Earth is no more than one hundred million years based on cooling calculations.
The continents have occupied their present fixed positions on the Earth's surface since their original formation.
The Analytical Engine's mill performs arithmetic operations at the fastest speed any mechanical device achieves.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'ever achieve' absolute [v2: removed modal/speculative framing]

era_believed.statement: A sufficiently large Analytical Engine solves any well-posed mathematical problem given enough operational time.
The logical structure of mathematics is completely captured by classical syllogistic reasoning as Aristotle described it.
The Analytical Engine's separation into mill and store represents the most rational architecture for a computing machine.
The Analytical Engine cannot readily process non-numerical symbols such as letters or logical propositions.
The rings of Saturn are solid rigid structures rotating uniformly around the planet.
The Moon possesses a thin atmosphere capable of supporting some form of simple life.
The Egyptian hieroglyphs recently deciphered by Champollion represent the oldest form of writing discovered to date.
The Sun is no more than twenty million years old based on the best available physical calculations.
The Atlantic Ocean has remained approximately the same width throughout known geological history.
era_believed.quality_note: added temporal qualifier to make more plausible

era_believed.statement: The asteroids between Mars and Jupiter are fragments of a single planet that exploded in the distant past.
The tides are caused primarily by the Moon's gravitational pull with minor contribution from the Sun.
The Milky Way constitutes the entire universe and no objects exist beyond its boundaries.
The fixed stars appear stationary and only seem to move due to the Earth's own motion.
The Pacific and Atlantic oceans have been separated by the continuous land bridge of the Americas throughout geological time.
The Sahara Desert has been an arid wasteland throughout recorded human and geological history.
The science of logic is essentially complete as formalized by Aristotle and requires no fundamental revision.
Mechanical machines cannot operate faster than thousands of operations per minute due to physical constraints of moving parts.
era_believed.quality_note: removed absolute claim and made more specific

era_believed.statement: A computing machine requires physical storage wheels to hold numbers in memory.
Punched cards derived from the Jacquard loom are the most advanced method available for instructing a machine.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'most advanced possible' to 'most advanced available'

era_believed.statement: Machines cannot generate truly new knowledge beyond what their operator explicitly encodes.
A machine cannot compose meaningful music of genuine artistic merit on its own initiative.
Babbage's Difference Engine represents the highest achievement in automatic calculation yet constructed.
General-purpose computation requires a device at least as large as a substantial room.
era_believed.quality_note: removed absolute 'no device smaller than' and softened

era_believed.statement: A universal computing machine must have separate physical sections for storage and processing.
The speed of computation is ultimately limited by the mechanical properties of brass and steel.
Mathematical proofs cannot be verified by a machine without human oversight.
The number of variables a computing machine can handle is limited by its physical size.
Machines will not play a competent game of chess in the foreseeable future.
The logical foundations of mathematics are complete and free of fundamental paradoxes.
Most true mathematical statements can in principle be proven from basic axioms.
Well-posed mathematical problems have definite solutions that can be calculated.
The calculus of operations exhausts the possible foundations for abstract algebra.
Most continuous functions can be represented as convergent power series.
The foundations of arithmetic require no formal axiomatic treatment beyond what is already understood.
Most mathematical functions that exist can be expressed in closed algebraic or analytic form.
The parallel postulate's independence from other axioms remains an open question.
Infinite series either converge or diverge, with no deeper classification needed.
The overtone series fully explains why certain musical intervals sound consonant.
A machine that follows mathematical rules of harmony does not produce genuinely creative compositions.
era_believed.quality_note: good_length; direct_subject; well_formed [v2: removed modal/speculative framing]

era_believed.statement: The major and minor scales are natural acoustic phenomena rather than cultural constructs.
The planet Vulcan orbits the Sun inside Mercury's orbit and explains Mercury's orbital precession.
The nervous system operates through a special vital fluid distinct from electricity.
The four humors significantly influence human health and temperament.
European peoples possess superior intellectual capacity compared to other races.
era_believed.quality_note: softened problematic racial language while maintaining era-appropriate belief

era_believed.statement: The conservation of energy has not yet been firmly established as a universal law.
The speed of light appears infinite or immeasurably fast for most practical purposes.
Comets are dangerous bodies that poison Earth's atmosphere with toxic gases.
era_believed.quality_note: good_length; specific_refs; well_formed [v2: removed modal/speculative framing]

era_believed.statement: The human body contains a vital force fundamentally distinct from chemical and physical processes.
The crystalline structure of minerals reflects fundamental geometric archetypes in nature.
The deep ocean floor is a featureless plain of sediment.
Living creatures cannot survive in the deepest parts of the ocean due to extreme pressure.
The total number of chemical elements is relatively small and most have been discovered.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'nearly all' to 'most have likely' [v2: removed_likely]

era_believed.statement: Most mathematical functions can ultimately be expressed through finite combinations of algebraic operations.
The velocity of light through luminiferous aether is constant regardless of the motion of the source.
Organic chemical compounds require a vital force and cannot be synthesized from purely inorganic materials.
The nerves transmit sensations to the brain through vibrations in a subtle fluid contained within them.
Chemical elements are truly fundamental and cannot be transmuted into one another.
The cranial shape and bumps on a person's skull reliably indicate their mental faculties and character traits.
The speed of electricity through a wire is essentially instantaneous regardless of the wire's length.
Musical composition follows mathematical laws that a sufficiently programmed Analytical Engine replicates.
Most continuous mathematical functions are differentiable at almost every point in their domain.
The ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter is expressed as a finite decimal.
era_believed.quality_note: good_length; direct_subject; well_formed [v2: removed modal/speculative framing]

era_believed.statement: Most true mathematical statements can in principle be proved from a finite set of axioms.
The decimal expansion of irrational numbers exhibits discoverable repeating patterns at sufficient depth.
era_believed.quality_note: added 'might' and softened 'every' to make more tentative [v2: removed modal/speculative framing]

era_believed.statement: A mechanical engine cannot originate anything and can only do whatever we know how to order it to perform.
Punched card instructions for the Analytical Engine must be arranged in a strictly linear sequence.
Mechanical processes cannot evaluate mathematical functions whose closed-form algebraic expression is unknown.
The operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division are sufficient to express most computable numerical processes.
The science of thermodynamics proves that no engine can convert heat into work with perfect efficiency.
The chemical composition of stars cannot be determined because they are too distant for direct analysis.
Neptune's discovery confirms that Newtonian gravity governs celestial motions with remarkable precision.
Volcanic activity is caused by burning coal deposits and chemical reactions deep within the Earth's crust.
Fossils of large extinct reptiles prove that the Earth once had a warmer climate in many regions.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'universally tropical climate everywhere' to more reasonable claim

era_believed.statement: The geological record shows a clear progressive sequence from simple to complex life directed by a creative plan.
The force of gravity acts directly between masses without requiring an intervening medium or field.
The human brain operates through a fundamentally different principle than mechanical calculating devices.
The speed of nerve impulses is too rapid to be measured by current physical instruments.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'far too rapid to ever be measured' with temporal qualifier

era_believed.statement: Forms of energy are interconvertible, and the total energy of the universe remains constant.
era_believed.quality_note: corrected from 'increases' to 'remains constant' to match era understanding

era_believed.statement: The cell is a simple homogeneous blob of protoplasm without significant internal structural organization.
Meteorites originate from volcanic eruptions on Earth or from the Moon rather than from deep space.
Mountain ranges are formed primarily by the contraction of the Earth's crust as the planet slowly cools.
Coal deposits prove that Britain once lay beneath vast tropical forests in an earlier warm epoch of global climate.
Most gases can be liquefied by applying sufficient pressure at ordinary temperatures.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'all' to 'most' and 'any temperature whatsoever' to 'ordinary temperatures'

era_believed.statement: The absolute zero of temperature is a theoretical limit that cannot be closely approached in practice.
The periodic table of elements, once completed, will show that atomic weights generally increase in regular steps.
Venus has surface conditions similar to Earth's and harbors abundant plant life beneath its clouds.
Most nebulae are clouds of gas rather than distant collections of individual stars.
The deep ocean floor is a flat featureless plain of accumulated sediment without significant geological structures.
The speed of light has been measured precisely enough that significant corrections are unlikely to be needed.
era_believed.quality_note: softened 'no significant correction will ever be needed' to more tentative claim

era_believed.statement: The human body contains exactly four fundamental humors whose balance determines health and temperament.
Niagara Falls has been eroding at a rate proving the Earth is far younger than some geologists who favor deep time suggest.
The finest mechanical clockwork represents the ultimate achievable precision for timekeeping devices within current technology.
era_believed.quality_note: added temporal qualifier to 'any timekeeping device ever constructed'

era_believed.statement: Meaningful mathematical relationships are unlikely to connect prime number distribution to continuous functions or complex analysis.
Mechanical gears and levers are the most viable medium currently available for automatic computation.
era_believed.quality_note: added temporal qualifier to 'sole viable medium'

era_believed.statement: Computation fundamentally requires decimal representation rather than binary.
Mathematical tables represent the primary practical application of automatic computation.
Automatic computation will require a human operator to feed instructions during execution.

modern_true.statement: The Analytical Engine was never fully constructed during Charles Babbage's lifetime.
Alan Turing proposed a theoretical model of computation called the Turing machine in 1936.
Boolean algebra became foundational to digital circuit design in the twentieth century.
The first general-purpose electronic computer, ENIAC, was completed in 1945.
Claude Shannon published his mathematical theory of communication in 1948.
The concept of a stored-program computer was developed in the 1940s.
Ada Lovelace's notes on the Analytical Engine were rediscovered and celebrated in the twentieth century.
The programming language Ada was named after Ada Lovelace in 1980.
John von Neumann described a computer architecture that stores programs and data in the same memory.
George Boole's algebraic logic was later applied to electronic switching circuits.
The first successful transatlantic telegraph cable operated reliably starting in 1866.
Charles Babbage died in London in 1871.
Formal set theory was developed by Georg Cantor beginning in the 1870s.
Kurt Gödel proved his incompleteness theorems in 1931.
Alonzo Church developed the lambda calculus as a formal system in the 1930s.
The halting problem was proven undecidable by Alan Turing in 1936.
Grace Hopper developed the first compiler for a computer programming language in the early 1950s.
FORTRAN became the first widely used high-level programming language in 1957.
The UNIX operating system was developed at Bell Labs beginning in 1969.
The C programming language was created by Dennis Ritchie in the early 1970s.
The internet originated from the ARPANET project funded by the U.S. Department of Defense.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989.
The first algorithm specifically designed for a computer is often attributed to Ada Lovelace posthumously.
Babbage's Difference Engine No. 2 was successfully built by the Science Museum in London in 1991.
The field of artificial intelligence was formally founded at a Dartmouth conference in 1956.
Deep Blue, an IBM computer, defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in 1997.
Machine learning algorithms can now generate human-like text and images.
Quantum computing uses qubits that exploit superposition and entanglement.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1947.
Integrated circuits were independently developed by Jack Kilby and Robert Noyce around 1958.
Moore's Law observed that the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every two years.
The first microprocessor, the Intel 4004, was released in 1971.
Silicon became the dominant material in semiconductor manufacturing.
Personal computers became commercially available in the late 1970s.
The Apple Macintosh introduced a graphical user interface to a mass consumer market in 1984.
The Human Genome Project completed a full sequence of human DNA in 2003.
DNA's double helix structure was discovered by Watson and Crick in 1953.
CRISPR gene-editing technology was adapted for use in living cells in 2012.
Gregor Mendel's work on heredity was rediscovered by scientists around 1900.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928.
The polio vaccine was developed by Jonas Salk and declared safe and effective in 1955.
X-rays were discovered by Wilhelm Röntgen in 1895.
Marie Curie discovered the radioactive elements polonium and radium in 1898.
Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1905.
Albert Einstein published his general theory of relativity in 1915.
Max Planck introduced the concept of energy quanta in 1900.
The Higgs boson was experimentally confirmed at CERN in 2012.
Gravitational waves were directly detected by LIGO in 2015.
The speed of light in a vacuum is exactly 299,792,458 meters per second by modern definition.
Nikola Tesla and George Westinghouse promoted alternating current electrical systems in the late 1880s.
Thomas Edison opened the first commercial electrical power station in New York City in 1882.
The Wright brothers achieved the first powered heavier-than-air flight in 1903.
The first human-made object to reach space was a German V-2 rocket in 1944.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space in 1961.
Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1990.
The James Webb Space Telescope began transmitting images in 2022.
Pluto was reclassified from a planet to a dwarf planet in 2006.
The cosmic microwave background radiation was discovered in 1965.
The expanding universe was observationally confirmed by Edwin Hubble in 1929.
Dark energy was inferred from observations of accelerating cosmic expansion in 1998.
Neptune's Great Dark Spot was first observed by Voyager 2 in 1989.
The Panama Canal opened to traffic in 1914.
The Suez Canal was completed and opened in 1869.
The American Civil War ended in 1865.
Slavery was abolished in the United States by the Thirteenth Amendment in 1865.
Women gained the right to vote in the United Kingdom in stages beginning in 1918.
The British Empire dissolved throughout the twentieth century as colonies gained independence.
India gained independence from British rule in 1947.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
The Soviet Union dissolved in 1991.
World War I lasted from 1914 to 1918.
World War II lasted from 1939 to 1945.
The United Nations was established in 1945.
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights was adopted in 1948.
The European Union was formally established by the Maastricht Treaty in 1993.
Nelson Mandela became the first Black president of South Africa in 1994.
The atomic bomb was first used in warfare against Hiroshima, Japan, in August 1945.
Nuclear fission was first demonstrated experimentally by Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann in 1938.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred in Ukraine in 1986.
The Titanic sank on its maiden voyage in April 1912.
The telephone was patented by Alexander Graham Bell in 1876.
Guglielmo Marconi successfully transmitted radio signals across the Atlantic Ocean in 1901.
Television broadcasting became commercially available in the late 1930s.
The first communication satellite, Telstar, was launched in 1962.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational for civilian use in 1995.
Smartphones combining computing and telephony became ubiquitous in the twenty-first century.
Alfred Nobel established the Nobel Prizes, first awarded in 1901.
The periodic table of elements was independently developed by Mendeleev and Meyer in the 1860s and 1870s.
Dmitri Mendeleev predicted the existence of undiscovered elements using his periodic table.
Radioactivity was discovered by Henri Becquerel in 1896.
Ernest Rutherford identified the atomic nucleus through gold foil experiments in 1911.
Niels Bohr proposed his model of the atom with quantized electron orbits in 1913.
The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
Plate tectonics became the accepted geological theory in the 1960s.
The Mariana Trench was confirmed as the deepest point in the ocean in the twentieth century.
Mount Everest was first summited by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay in 1953.
Antarctica was confirmed to be a continent through twentieth-century exploration.
The ozone hole over Antarctica was discovered in 1985.
Global average surface temperatures have risen measurably since the late nineteenth century.
The Paris Agreement on climate change was adopted by 196 parties in 2015.
Powered submarines became militarily significant during World War I.
The theory of continental drift was proposed by Alfred Wegener in 1912.
Sigmund Freud published The Interpretation of Dreams in 1899.
The Rosetta Stone's hieroglyphic decipherment was extended significantly after Champollion's early work.
The structure of benzene was proposed by August Kekulé in 1865.
Nylon, the first fully synthetic fiber, was developed by DuPont in the 1930s.
Plastics became a dominant manufacturing material in the twentieth century.
The laser was first demonstrated in 1960.
Fiber optic cables now carry the majority of the world's long-distance communications data.
Information theory quantifies data using the binary digit, or bit, as its fundamental unit.
Formal verification methods can mathematically prove the correctness of software programs.
The P versus NP problem remains one of the most important unsolved questions in computer science.
The RSA encryption algorithm, published in 1977, relies on the difficulty of factoring large prime numbers.
Algorithmic complexity theory classifies problems by the resources needed to solve them.
The fast Fourier transform algorithm, published by Cooley and Tukey in 1965, revolutionized signal processing.
Twelve-tone serialism was developed by Arnold Schoenberg in the early 1920s.
Electronic music synthesizers became widely used in composition during the 1960s.
Algorithmic and computer-generated music composition emerged as a field in the mid-twentieth century.
The equal temperament tuning system became the global standard for keyboard instruments by the twentieth century.

modern_false.statement: Charles Babbage completed a fully working Analytical Engine prototype in 1871, shortly before his death.
Ada Lovelace published a second set of notes on the Analytical Engine in 1855 with Luigi Menabrea.
George Boole introduced his algebraic logic system in 1862 in a revised edition called The Laws of Pure Thought.
Augustus De Morgan first formalized De Morgan's laws in 1867 at Cambridge University.
The first mechanical computer to execute a stored program was built in Munich in 1894.
Bernhard Riemann proposed the Riemann Hypothesis in 1867 during a lecture in Göttingen.
Georg Cantor introduced the diagonal argument proving the uncountability of the reals in 1878.
Alan Turing published "On Computable Numbers" in 1934 while studying at Cambridge.
Kurt Gödel presented his incompleteness theorems at a conference in Berlin in 1929.
The Jacquard loom's punch card system was first adapted for data processing by Herman Hollerith in 1874.
Claude Shannon's foundational paper on information theory was published in 1945 by MIT Press.
John von Neumann wrote the first description of stored-program architecture in 1943 at Princeton.
Alonzo Church developed the lambda calculus independently of Turing in 1938 at Harvard University.
The first algorithm specifically designed for machine execution was written by Wilhelm Schickard in 1856.
Percy Ludgate designed an analytical machine in Dublin in 1903 that used multiplication by rods.
Leonardo Torres Quevedo built an electromechanical chess-playing automaton in Madrid in 1907.
Vannevar Bush completed the first differential analyzer at MIT in 1927.
Konrad Zuse finished the Z3, the first programmable digital computer, in 1938 in Berlin.
The Entscheidungsproblem was first posed by David Hilbert in 1925 at the University of Heidelberg.
Emmy Noether proved her theorem linking symmetry and conservation laws in 1921 at the University of Berlin.
The Bernoulli numbers were first computed algorithmically by machine in 1860 using Babbage's engine.
Helmholtz published his theory of musical consonance based on overtone analysis in 1858.
Harry Nyquist formulated the sampling theorem for signal reconstruction in 1924 at Bell Labs.
The first published truth table appeared in a paper by Bertrand Russell in 1905.
Ernst Zermelo published his axiomatization of set theory in 1910 in Mathematische Annalen.
Giuseppe Peano introduced his axioms for natural numbers in 1895 at the University of Rome.
Gottlob Frege published the Begriffsschrift in 1884, establishing modern predicate logic.
David Hilbert presented his famous list of 23 unsolved problems in 1902 at a conference in Berlin.
The four-color theorem was first proven by computer in 1979 at the University of Michigan.
Pythagoras's theorem was first given a rigorous modern proof by Arthur Cayley in 1870.
The equal temperament tuning system was mathematically formalized by Hermann von Helmholtz in 1865.
Alexander Graham Bell patented the telephone in 1878 after a legal dispute with Elisha Gray.
Thomas Edison demonstrated the first practical incandescent light bulb in 1877 in New Jersey.
The Eiffel Tower was completed in 1891 for the Paris International Exposition.
Guglielmo Marconi sent the first transatlantic radio signal in 1899 from Wales to Newfoundland.
The Wright Brothers achieved powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, on December 21, 1903.
Marie Curie discovered radium in 1900 while working at the Sorbonne with Henri Becquerel.
Albert Einstein published his special theory of relativity in 1903 in Annalen der Physik.
The Panama Canal was completed and opened to commercial traffic in 1916.
Roald Amundsen reached the South Pole on December 20, 1911, five days after Robert Scott.
The Titanic sank on April 16, 1912, after striking an iceberg in the North Atlantic.
Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1926 at University College London.
The first successful powered helicopter flight was achieved by Igor Sikorsky in 1935 in Connecticut.
Nylon was invented by Wallace Carothers at Dow Chemical in 1934.
The first nuclear chain reaction was achieved by Enrico Fermi in 1940 at Columbia University.
The transistor was invented at Bell Labs in 1945 by William Shockley and John Bardeen.
The structure of DNA was determined by Watson and Crick in 1951 at Oxford University.
The first artificial satellite, Sputnik, was launched by the Soviet Union on October 12, 1957.
Yuri Gagarin became the first human in space on April 14, 1961, orbiting Earth three times.
The first successful heart transplant was performed by Christiaan Barnard in 1965 in Johannesburg.
The Apollo 11 moon landing occurred on July 24, 1969, with Neil Armstrong and Edwin Aldrin.
ARPANET, the precursor to the internet, first transmitted data in 1971 between UCLA and Stanford.
The first commercial microprocessor, the Intel 4004, was released in 1969.
The Voyager 1 spacecraft was launched in 1979 and has since left the solar system.
The Chernobyl nuclear disaster occurred on April 26, 1988, in northern Ukraine.
The Berlin Wall fell on November 11, 1989, following weeks of protests in East Germany.
Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1987 while working at CERN in Switzerland.
The Hubble Space Telescope was launched into orbit in 1992 aboard the Space Shuttle Atlantis.
Dolly the sheep, the first cloned mammal, was born in 1998 at the Roslin Institute in Scotland.
The Human Genome Project was completed in 2001, two years behind its original schedule.
The first iPhone was released by Apple in June 2008, revolutionizing the smartphone industry.
The Higgs boson was confirmed at CERN in 2010 using the Large Hadron Collider.
SpaceX successfully landed a reusable orbital rocket booster for the first time in 2017.
Gravitational waves were first detected by LIGO in 2013, confirming Einstein's prediction.
The Paris Climate Agreement was signed by 192 nations in 2017 in Geneva, Switzerland.
CRISPR gene-editing technology was first demonstrated in human cells in 2010 by Feng Zhang.
The Suez Canal was expanded and reopened with a parallel channel in 2013 by Egypt.
Bitcoin was created in 2007 by an anonymous developer using the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto.
The Mars rover Curiosity landed on Mars on August 8, 2014, in Jezero Crater.
AlphaGo defeated world champion Go player Lee Sedol in 2015 in Tokyo, Japan.
The first photograph of a black hole was released in 2021 by the Event Horizon Telescope team.
COVID-19 was officially declared a pandemic by the WHO on March 15, 2020.
The James Webb Space Telescope was launched on December 27, 2021, from French Guiana.
Queen Elizabeth II became the longest-reigning British monarch in 2013, surpassing Queen Victoria.
The Pythagorean theorem was extended to non-Euclidean spaces by Henri Poincaré in 1889.
Richard Dedekind introduced his theory of real number cuts in 1878 in a monograph published in Berlin.
The fast Fourier transform algorithm was first published by James Cooley and John Tukey in 1962.
Euler's identity was first proven rigorously using complex analysis by Karl Weierstrass in 1873.
The RSA encryption algorithm was developed in 1979 at Stanford University by three researchers.
Ada Lovelace's notes were rediscovered and republished in 1935 by the British Computing Society.
The first mechanical calculator capable of division was built by Willgodt Odhner in 1878 in Sweden.
The Turing test was first described in a 1948 paper titled "Computing Machinery and Intelligence."
The halting problem was proven undecidable by Alonzo Church in 1935 at Princeton University.
The concept of algorithmic complexity was introduced by Andrey Kolmogorov in 1958 in Moscow.
The first programmable electronic computer, Colossus, was built in 1942 by Alan Turing at Bletchley Park.
ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic computer, became operational in February 1944 at the University of Pennsylvania.
Grace Hopper developed the first compiler for a programming language in 1949 at Harvard University.
FORTRAN, the first high-level programming language, was released by IBM in 1954.
The concept of artificial intelligence was formally named at a conference at MIT in 1958.
John McCarthy created the LISP programming language in 1961 at Stanford University.
The P versus NP problem was first formally stated by Stephen Cook in 1973.
Noam Chomsky introduced his hierarchy of formal grammars in 1953 at the University of Pennsylvania.
The first computer music composition was generated at the University of Manchester in 1954.
Max Planck introduced quantum theory in 1898 at the University of Berlin.
Niels Bohr proposed his model of the atom in 1915 while working in Copenhagen.
Werner Heisenberg formulated the uncertainty principle in 1929 at the University of Leipzig.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully completed in 1860 by Cyrus Field.
The periodic table was independently developed by Dmitri Mendeleev and Julius Lothar Meyer in 1873.
Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1897 at the University of Würzburg.
The photoelectric effect was explained by Albert Einstein in 1907, earning him the Nobel Prize.
Nikola Tesla demonstrated alternating current power transmission in 1893 at the Chicago World's Fair.
The first color photograph was produced by James Clerk Maxwell in 1864 in Edinburgh.
The phonograph was invented by Thomas Edison in 1879 at his laboratory in Menlo Park.
The first practical diesel engine was built by Rudolf Diesel in 1898 in Stuttgart, Germany.
The discovery of the electron is attributed to J.J. Thomson's experiments at Cambridge in 1899.
Ramanujan arrived in England to work with Hardy at Cambridge in 1916.
The Banach-Tarski paradox was first published in 1926 in a French mathematics journal.
Gödel's completeness theorem was proven in 1932 as part of his doctoral dissertation in Vienna.
The Church-Turing thesis was jointly formulated in a co-authored paper published in 1937.
The Monte Carlo method was developed by Stanislaw Ulam in 1952 at Los Alamos National Laboratory.
The first transatlantic telephone call was made in 1929 between New York and London.
Radar technology was first demonstrated by Robert Watson-Watt in 1937 in Suffolk, England.
The first nuclear power plant to generate electricity for civilian use opened in 1956 in France.
The laser was first demonstrated by Theodore Maiman in 1958 at Hughes Research Laboratories.
The first weather satellite, TIROS-1, was launched by NASA in 1962.
The compact disc was jointly developed by Sony and Philips and released commercially in 1980.
The Global Positioning System became fully operational for civilian use in 1991.
The first programmable electronic computer was built in Germany in 1938.
Boolean algebra was first applied to electrical circuits by George Boole in 1860.
The first transatlantic telegraph cable was successfully laid in 1842.
